factual-accuracy.requirement: Explicitly states no published head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy studies exist as of query date
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states: “No direct, head-to-head studies comparing the diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, or clinical utility of DSM-5 vs. ICD-11 for ASD exist as of April 2026,” which directly satisfies the requirement that no published head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy studies exist as of the query date.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 requires all 3 social-communication subdomains (social-emotional reciprocity, nonverbal behaviors, relationships)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under “Core social-communication criteria” that DSM-5 “requires all 3 specific deficits: social-emotional reciprocity; nonverbal communication; relationships” and reiterates that “all three” sub-criteria are required.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 uses examples-based approach for social communication/reciprocity rather than fixed checklist
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes ICD-11 as “less rigid,” “broader,” and allowing “greater clinical flexibility,” but it does not explicitly state that ICD-11 uses an examples-based approach for social communication/reciprocity rather than a fixed checklist.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 requires at least 2 of 4 restricted and repetitive behavior categories and explicitly includes hyper- or hyporeactivity to sensory input as one category.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states DSM-5 includes sensory hyper- or hyporeactivity as item B4 within the four RRB categories and notes that two RRB types are required, implying “2 of 4.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 includes sensory reactivity within RRB domain as examples without numeric threshold
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that ICD-11 includes sensory features (e.g., “unusual sensory sensitivities or interests,” hyper-/hyposensitivity) as exemplars within the RRB domain and indicates they are not mandatory. It does not provide any numeric threshold for sensory reactivity, describing it only as examples/manifestations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States DSM-5 uses three severity levels (Level 1, 2, 3) based on support needed
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that DSM-5 has three severity levels and explicitly describes them as based on the degree of support required (e.g., “requiring support,” “substantial support,” “very substantial support”). It also labels them as Level 1–3 in the severity/support specification section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ICD-11 uses subcategories by intellectual development and language impairment (codes 6A02.0-6A02.5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that ICD-11 ASD uses sub-codes based on intellectual developmental disorder and functional language impairment, and explicitly mentions the code range “6A02.0–6A02.3” (and elsewhere “6A02.0–6A02.3 cover the main specifier combinations”). It does not state the full range “6A02.0–6A02.5” required by the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both require symptoms present in early developmental period but may manifest later when demands exceed capacities or be masked
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states for DSM-5 that symptoms must be present in the early developmental period but may be masked or first noted when demands exceed capacities, and for ICD-11 that onset is in the developmental period with delayed/masked presentation due to compensation recognized.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both require clinically significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important functioning areas
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that DSM-5 requires deficits that “cause clinically significant impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning” and that ICD-11 requires symptoms resulting in “significant impairment in personal, family, social, educational, occupational, or other important areas of functioning.” This covers both manuals’ functional impairment thresholds.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Cites Kulage et al. 2019 (or similar 2019 meta-analysis) showing approximately 20-21% decrease in DSM-5 diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not cite Kulage et al. (2019) or any similar 2019 meta-analysis, nor does it report an approximately 20–21% decrease in DSM-5 diagnoses versus DSM-IV-TR. It discusses DSM-5 vs ICD-11 and mentions sensitivity/specificity generally, but the specific 2019 meta-analytic finding is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Includes at least 1 systematic review or meta-analysis with study design, sample size, and pooled statistics specified
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “systematic reviews” in general terms but does not identify any specific systematic review or meta-analysis with an explicit study design, sample size, and pooled (meta-analytic) statistics. No pooled effect estimates (e.g., pooled sensitivity/specificity with CI) are reported for any cited source.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Includes at least 1 primary empirical study (e.g., Wiggins et al. 2019 SEED2 n=1,061 or similar) with sample size and sensitivity/specificity data
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not include any primary empirical study with a stated sample size alongside sensitivity and specificity values. It cites a PubMed item about DSM-5 sensitivity/specificity but does not report the study’s n or the sensitivity/specificity numbers, and it provides no DSM-5 vs ICD-11 primary accuracy study at all.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports GRADE quality ratings (High/Moderate/Low/Very Low) for at least 2 sources with justification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not report any GRADE quality ratings (High/Moderate/Low/Very Low) for any sources, nor does it provide GRADE-style justifications. It mentions evidence gaps and lists sources, but it never assigns GRADE ratings to at least two sources with justification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes available evidence compares DSM-5 vs DSM-IV or discusses ICD-11 conceptually rather than head-to-head testing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that no direct head-to-head DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy studies exist and that most available evidence compares DSM-5 vs DSM-IV-TR or ICD-10, framing ICD-11 discussion as conceptual/expert projection rather than empirical testing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO released ICD-11 CDDR in March 2024 with ASD code 6A02
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions ICD-11 ASD as code 6A02, but it does not state that WHO released the ICD-11 CDDR in March 2024. It also does not connect any March 2024 CDDR release to ASD code 6A02.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO ICD-11 includes atypical sensory reactivity within restricted/inflexible RRB domain
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that ICD-11 explicitly includes “unusual sensory sensitivities or interests” within the restricted, repetitive, or inflexible behavior domain, describing it as part of the RRB domain exemplars.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States APA DSM-5 requires all 3 social-communication subdomains and at least 2 of 4 RRBs
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states DSM-5 “requires all 3 specific deficits” in social-communication and that “Two RRBs (of four, including B4) are required,” which matches the requirement of all 3 social-communication subdomains and at least 2 of 4 RRBs.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States AAP 2020 clinical report references DSM-5 for US practice
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions the AAP and cites a 2020 Pediatrics article, but it does not state that the AAP 2020 clinical report references DSM-5 for US practice. There is no explicit linkage that the AAP report uses DSM-5 as the diagnostic framework in the US.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States AAP notes limited/inconclusive evidence for standalone sensory processing disorder diagnosis
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses AAP guidance on recognizing sensory processing differences in ASD but does not mention AAP’s position that evidence is limited or inconclusive for diagnosing standalone sensory processing disorder. It never references SPD as a separate diagnosis or notes AAP caution about its diagnostic validity.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NICE or NHS England 2024 framework specifies ICD-11 criteria for primary clinical description
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state that a NICE or NHS England 2024 framework specifies ICD-11 criteria for the primary clinical description. It mentions NICE guidance and NHS rollout generally, but provides no 2024 NICE/NHS England framework claim tying ICD-11 to “primary clinical description.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NHS requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during transition period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the NHS accepts DSM-5 and ICD-11 and that ICD-11 is being rolled out, but it does not state that the NHS requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during a transition period. There is no mention of an ICD-10-to-ICD-11 mapping requirement for national datasets or any explicit transition-period mandate.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States ASAN supported inclusive unified spectrum diagnosis accounting for masking and compensation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that ASAN pushed for a unified autism diagnosis and inclusion of masking/compensation and sensory differences in DSM-5, aligning with an inclusive unified spectrum diagnosis accounting for masking and compensation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States National Autistic Society presents both DSM-5 and ICD-11 as largely similar regarding core characteristics
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the National Autistic Society “supports the use of either DSM-5 or ICD-11 criteria,” implying they are both valid frameworks and broadly aligned on core features. It does not explicitly state that NAS presents DSM-5 and ICD-11 as “largely similar regarding core characteristics.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NAS uses identity-affirming or neurodiversity-affirming language
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes NAS positions using affirming framing (e.g., “sensory needs,” “support needs,” “understanding the impact,” and “improved pathways”) rather than deficit-only language, indicating identity-/neurodiversity-affirming language when discussing NAS.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Autism Speaks provides DSM-5 text but does not take formal position on DSM vs ICD preference
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says Autism Speaks publicizes DSM-5 and ICD-11 and “does not take an official stance” on which manual is more neurodiversity-affirming, but it does not state that Autism Speaks provides DSM-5 text. It also does not explicitly say Autism Speaks has no formal preference between DSM and ICD.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States meta-analysis or empirical evidence shows DSM-5 resulted in approximately 20-21% decrease in diagnoses vs DSM-IV-TR
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses DSM-5 vs DSM-IV-TR sensitivity/specificity and mentions diagnostic shifts, but it does not state that meta-analysis or empirical evidence shows DSM-5 caused an approximately 20–21% decrease in diagnoses versus DSM-IV-TR. No such percentage decrease claim appears anywhere in the response.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes DSM-5 showed reduced sensitivity for PDD-NOS, Asperger profiles, or cognitively able subgroups
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses DSM-5 potentially being less sensitive and missing subtle or high-functioning cases, but it does not specifically note reduced sensitivity for PDD-NOS, Asperger profiles, or cognitively able subgroups. It mentions DSM-IV-TR comparisons and “high-functioning” generally without explicitly tying this to PDD-NOS/Asperger/cognitively able groups as required.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes ICD-11 breadth and compensation language may increase false positives or over-diagnosis (concern awaiting empirical testing)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly notes that ICD-11’s broader, more flexible criteria could increase diagnostic heterogeneity and “risk of false positives,” and frames this as expert projection “not empirically confirmed,” i.e., awaiting empirical testing. It also mentions masking/compensation language in ICD-11, but does not clearly tie compensation language itself to increased false positives.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States sensory features have high sensitivity but lower specificity for ASD (occur in other neurodevelopmental conditions)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses DSM-5 being more specific but less sensitive and mentions sensory-dominant presentations, but it does not state that sensory features have high sensitivity and lower specificity for ASD or that they commonly occur in other neurodevelopmental conditions. It also does not explicitly compare sensory features’ sensitivity vs. specificity.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States US uses ICD-10-CM code F84.0 for autism spectrum disorder
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that US billing relies on ICD-10-CM code F84.0 for ASD in the “Coding and Reimbursement: US and UK” section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States US has no official ICD-11-CM implementation date or transition timeline announced
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says “ICD-11 is not yet implemented for billing” in the US and speculates about a late-2020s transition, but it does not explicitly state that the US has no official ICD-11-CM implementation date or transition timeline announced. It also does not mention ICD-11-CM specifically or the lack of an announced official timeline.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States NHS England uses ICD-11 for clinical description of autism, noting that the transition from ICD-10 to ICD-11 is underway.
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that in the UK the NHS accepts ICD-11 and that “ICD-11 is being rolled out nationally,” implying an ongoing transition from ICD-10 to ICD-11 for clinical description/coding.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States UK still requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during transition
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses UK/NHS accepting DSM-5 and ICD-11 and mentions ICD-11 rollout, but it does not state that the UK still requires ICD-10 mapping for national datasets during the transition. There is no explicit mention of continued ICD-10 dataset requirements or dual ICD-10/ICD-11 mapping for national reporting.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States WHO ICD-11 took effect January 1, 2022 for member states
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response claims ICD-11 is required for use in all WHO member states but does not state that ICD-11 took effect on January 1, 2022 for member states. No effective date is provided anywhere in the response, so the specific January 1, 2022 detail is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly flags absence of direct DSM-5 vs ICD-11 studies and distinguishes projections/inferences from empirical data
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states there are “No direct, head-to-head studies” comparing DSM-5 vs ICD-11 diagnostic accuracy and reiterates “No direct comparative data exists.” It also clearly labels downstream claims as “expert hypotheses or projections” rather than “verified empirical outcomes,” distinguishing inference from empirical evidence.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Quantifies diagnostic impact by subgroup (e.g., ~21% decrease overall, differential PDD-NOS/Asperger/cognitively able outcomes)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not quantify diagnostic impact by subgroup (e.g., an overall ~21% decrease) or provide differential outcomes for former DSM-IV subgroups like PDD-NOS vs Asperger’s vs cognitively able individuals. It discusses projected over/under-diagnosis qualitatively and notes a lack of head-to-head studies, but gives no subgroup-specific percentages or comparative reclassification rates.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Contrasts DSM-5 numeric RRB threshold (≥2 of 4) vs ICD-11 clinical judgment approach without fixed count
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states DSM-5 requires “two RRBs (of four)” and that ICD-11 requires “at least one” RRB and is more flexible, but it does not explicitly contrast DSM-5’s numeric threshold (≥2 of 4) against ICD-11’s clinical-judgment approach without a fixed count. It implies ICD-11 flexibility yet still presents a numeric minimum rather than emphasizing no fixed count.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes test accuracy studies from clinical utility perspectives and provides GRADE ratings with justification
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not distinguish diagnostic test accuracy evidence (e.g., sensitivity/specificity studies) from clinical utility outcomes, and it even claims no head-to-head accuracy studies exist while later citing a DSM-5 sensitivity/specificity paper. It also does not provide any GRADE ratings with explicit justifications per source, despite mentioning “GRADE quality rating” in the query.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Integrates sensory role, functional impairment, and social-communication + RRB dyad into actionable clinical decision guidance
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response links sensory features to the RRB domain and discusses functional impairment thresholds, then translates these differences into clinical implications (e.g., risk of missing sensory-dominant or masked cases under DSM-5 vs broader capture under ICD-11). It also provides decision-relevant guidance about how sensory presentations may satisfy the RRB requirement and how impairment/support needs affect diagnosis and services.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains disconnect between clinical ICD-11 adoption and administrative ICD-10 mapping/coding during transition
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts DSM-5/ICD-11 clinical diagnostic use with billing realities, noting US clinicians use DSM-5 while claims use ICD-10-CM F84.0 and that ICD-11 is not yet implemented for billing, requiring mapping/dual coding during transition. This describes the clinical-vs-administrative disconnect during ICD-11 adoption.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies ICD-11 breadth trading sensitivity for specificity as hypothesis requiring empirical validation rather than settled fact
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly frames ICD-11’s broader/flexible criteria as “expert hypotheses or projections” and repeatedly notes the lack of direct head-to-head validation data, cautioning that impacts on sensitivity/specificity are not empirically confirmed.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States DSM-5 and ICD-11 are essentially identical or interchangeable without acknowledging differences in RRB thresholds or severity systems
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes that DSM-5 and ICD-11 differ in RRB thresholds (DSM-5 requires ≥2 RRBs vs ICD-11 ≥1) and in severity systems (DSM-5 severity levels vs ICD-11 specifier subcodes). It characterizes them as “harmonized but structurally distinct,” not identical or interchangeable.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes response with six lettered sections (A through F) addressing each query component as specified
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections (e.g., 1–6) with an introduction and conclusion, not into six lettered sections A through F. It does not explicitly label or structure the content under headings “(A)” through “(F)” corresponding to each requested component.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria comparison in a table or structured side-by-side format for Section A
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes a side-by-side table comparing DSM-5 (2013) vs ICD-11 (2019) for Section A elements, with rows for core social-communication criteria, sensory processing mention/weighting, severity specifiers, age/onset requirements, and functional impairment thresholds.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise diagnostic and psychometric terminology (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, GRADE, diagnostic criteria)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses diagnostic/psychometric terminology such as “diagnostic accuracy,” “sensitivity,” “specificity,” “functional impairment,” “severity levels,” and “specifiers,” and it also references GRADE quality ratings in the evidence synthesis section.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains scholarly, evidence-based tone distinguishing empirical findings from clinical inference or opinion
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, academic tone and repeatedly distinguishes empirical evidence from expert projections (e.g., noting lack of head-to-head studies and labeling impacts as hypotheses). It frames uncertainty explicitly and avoids presenting speculation as established fact.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents DSM-5 vs ICD-11 criteria comparison as prose paragraphs without table or structured side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses structured side-by-side tables (e.g., the “Side-by-Side Comparison” table and the later “Domain” table) rather than presenting the DSM-5 vs ICD-11 comparison only as prose paragraphs.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing (e.g., 'sensory stuff', 'pretty similar', 'basically the same') instead of clinical terminology
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently uses formal clinical terminology (e.g., “restricted/repetitive behaviors,” “hyper- or hypo-reactivity,” “functional impairment,” “severity levels”) and does not use colloquial phrases like “sensory stuff,” “pretty similar,” or “basically the same.”

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official DSM-5 sources from APA, CDC, or cdc.gov/ncbddd/autism/hcp-dsm.html
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite any official DSM-5 sources from the APA or CDC, nor does it include the specified CDC URL (cdc.gov/ncbddd/autism/hcp-dsm.html). Its DSM-5 references are third-party sites (e.g., University of Washington PDF, private clinic, Embrace-Autism) rather than APA/CDC sources.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official ICD-11 sources from WHO CDDR (who.int) or pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov for ICD-11 clinical descriptions
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites an official WHO ICD-11 CDDR source on who.int (the WHO publication link) and also includes ICD-11-related clinical description articles hosted on pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites peer-reviewed medical/psychiatric journals via PubMed (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov), PMC, BMC Medicine, Molecular Psychiatry, or similar for evidence synthesis
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes peer-reviewed citations via PubMed (e.g., “Sensitivity and specificity of DSM-5 diagnostic criteria…” linked to pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov) and multiple PMC full-text articles linked to pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov, satisfying the requirement for journal evidence sources through these platforms.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites WHO ICD-11 implementation materials or clinical guidance from who.int
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites WHO ICD-11 implementation/clinical guidance materials hosted on who.int, including the WHO publication “Clinical descriptions and diagnostic requirements for ICD-11 ...” and a WHO autism fact sheet, both linked to who.int.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of NICE (nice.org.uk), AAP (publications.aap.org), NHS England (england.nhs.uk) for organizational positions
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites AAP sources from publications.aap.org (items 20 and 21) and a NICE source from nice.org.uk (item 22) when describing organizational guidance/positions.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 of ASAN (autisticadvocacy.org), National Autistic Society (autism.org.uk), Autism Speaks for advocacy perspectives
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites ASAN via an autisticadvocacy.org link (source 25), the National Autistic Society via autism.org.uk links (sources 23 and 26), and Autism Speaks via autismspeaks.org links (sources 7 and 27) in its advocacy section.

citation-quality.requirement: For evidence synthesis sources (systematic reviews, meta-analyses, primary studies), uses publications dated 2013 or later per query requirement
citation-quality.explanation: The response’s evidence synthesis sources are dated 2013 or later (e.g., 2021 PubMed article, 2023 Nature piece, 2022/2025 PMC/Springer items), and it does not cite pre-2013 systematic reviews/meta-analyses or primary studies for that section. While one listed item appears to be 2012 (ScienceDirect), it is not used as an evidence-synthesis DSM-5 vs ICD-11 comparative source.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Reddit, patient forums, WebMD comments, or social media posts as sources for diagnostic criteria or clinical evidence
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not cite Reddit, patient forums, WebMD comments, or social media posts as sources for diagnostic criteria or clinical evidence. All cited sources are organizational websites, journals (e.g., PubMed/PMC/Nature), or other web pages, but none are the specified social/forum platforms.
